titleStmt.title: Letter from Thomas Kirkpatrick to Captain George D. Mackintosh, 8 January 1918
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Thomas Kirkpatrick

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: From 1910 until 1954 Thomas Percy Kirkpatrick (1869-1954) served as the registrar for the Royal College of Physicians of Ireland. He also served as the general secretary of the Royal Academy of Medicine in Ireland. He took a particular interest in what were then termed venereal diseases; to encourage his patients (many of whom were prostitutes) to attend, he held a clinic for women at a discreet early morning hour to facilitate anonymity.

msIdentifier.repository: Royal College of Physicians of Ireland

correspAction.placeName: Royal College of Physicians, Kildare Street, Dublin, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
8th JANUARY 18. Capt. G.D.Mackintosh, R.A.M.C.,
Grain Fort,
Isle of Grain. Dear Sir, Your letter of December 28th, was considered by the
President and Fellows of the Royal College of Physicians of
Ireland at their meeting on Friday last. In reply I am directed to inform you that the admission
to the Membership of the College is rigidly controlled by
the By-Laws which may not be altered without sanction of the
Privy Council. The only By-Law under which exemption from any
part of the Examinations can be given is No. XVIII., p.8, (see
enclosure). This By-Law is intended to meet the case of persons
who have proved by their work their exceptional eminence in the
profession and a very strict scrutiny would be made of any claim
for exemption under it. It is, of course, open for anyone to claim
exemption, but such claim would not be admitted except under
very special circumstances. The provision of groups of subjects for the examination has
been adopted to meet the needs of persons who have been doing
special work. Candidates may apply for examination in subjects
under groups D (pp.10 and 11), and each application is
individually considered. I have to inform you further
that parts I. and II. of the examination may be taken
separately. I am,
Yours faithfully,
Fellow & Registrar.
